title: Blood Pressure
tagline: The silent killer you can control
oneLiner: Blood pressure is the force your blood exerts on artery walls. Too high for too long damages your heart, brain, and kidneys silently.

cards.title: What Do the Numbers Mean?
cards.body: BP is written as 120/80 mmHg. The top number (systolic) is pressure when your heart beats. The bottom (diastolic) is pressure when it rests. Normal is below 120/80. High BP (hypertension) starts at 130/80 or above.

cards.title: Silent Killer — No Symptoms
cards.body: High BP usually causes no symptoms for years while quietly damaging arteries, heart, kidneys, and eyes. Most people only discover it during a routine checkup — which is why regular monitoring matters.

cards.title: Salt and BP
cards.body: Salt (sodium) makes your body retain water, increasing blood volume, which raises pressure. Indians eat 8–10 g of salt daily — almost double the WHO recommendation of 5 g. Cutting salt is the fastest dietary fix.

cards.title: Potassium Opposes Sodium
cards.body: Potassium helps your kidneys flush out excess sodium and relaxes artery walls. Foods rich in potassium (bananas, coconut water, leafy greens, sweet potato) naturally counteract high salt intake.

cards.title: Stress Spikes BP Instantly
cards.body: Stress releases adrenaline and cortisol, which make your heart beat faster and blood vessels constrict. Chronic stress leads to chronic high BP. Managing stress is as important as cutting salt.

cards.title: Exercise Strengthens the Heart
cards.body: Regular aerobic exercise makes the heart more efficient so it pumps with less force, reducing resting BP by 5–8 mmHg — similar to some BP medications. Even brisk walking counts.

cards.title: Alcohol, Smoking & BP
cards.body: Smoking causes immediate BP spikes and damages artery lining. Alcohol raises BP, especially when consumed heavily. Both dramatically increase stroke risk when combined with hypertension.

doEat.name: Beetroot juice
doEat.note: Dietary nitrate converts to nitric oxide, relaxes arteries

dontEat.name: Caffeine in excess
dontEat.note: Temporary BP spike, especially in sensitive individuals

lifestyle: Reduce salt intake — stop adding extra salt at the table and switch to low-sodium cooking.
Monitor BP at home once a week. Record readings — patterns help your doctor more than a single reading.
Walk briskly 30 minutes most days — reduces BP 5–8 points.
Practice slow breathing or meditation for 10 minutes daily — proven to lower stress hormones.
Limit alcohol to no more than 1 drink/day; ideally quit completely.
Maintain a healthy weight — every 1 kg weight loss reduces systolic BP by about 1 mmHg.
Never stop BP medicines without consulting your doctor, even if you feel fine.
redFlags: Severe headache with no other cause, especially at the back of the head
Sudden vision changes or loss — BP damaging blood vessels in eyes
Sudden weakness or numbness on one side of the body — possible stroke
BP reading above 180/120 mmHg — hypertensive crisis, go to emergency immediately
Nosebleeds that keep recurring without injury

quiz.why: Below 120/80 is normal. 130/80 and above is considered high blood pressure (hypertension).

quiz.q: High blood pressure always causes headaches and symptoms.
quiz.why: High BP is known as the 'silent killer' — it rarely causes noticeable symptoms while quietly damaging organs for years.

quiz.q: Which mineral helps counteract excess sodium and lower BP?
quiz.why: Potassium helps kidneys flush out sodium and relaxes artery walls, directly opposing sodium's BP-raising effect.

quiz.q: Pickles (achar) are fine to eat daily if you have high BP.
quiz.why: Pickles are preserved with very large amounts of salt — one of the highest sodium foods in Indian diet. Avoid for high BP.

quiz.q: Regular aerobic exercise reduces resting BP by approximately:
quiz.why: Studies show consistent aerobic exercise reduces resting systolic BP by 5–8 mmHg — equivalent to some medications.
